title: Radians & the Unit Circle
blurb: Trigonometry escapes the triangle: angles measured by arc length, a circle of radius 1 where cosine and sine ARE the coordinates — and exact values in every quadrant.

lessons.title: Radians
lessons.concreteComparison: Measuring angles in degrees is like measuring your height in 'floors' — fine, but the unit is arbitrary (why 360?). Radians measure an angle by the most natural ruler a circle owns: its own radius, bent along the rim. One radius-length of arc = one radian. No committee chose it.
lessons.objective: Convert between degrees and radians, know the landmark angles in radian form, and use arc length s = rθ.
concept: **One radian** is the angle whose arc is exactly one radius long. Since the full rim is $2\pi r$, a full turn holds $2\pi$ radians: $360° = 2\pi$, so $\mathbf{180° = \pi}$ — the only conversion fact you need. One radian $\approx 57.3°$.

Convert by proportion: degrees $\to$ radians, multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$; radians $\to$ degrees, multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi}$. The landmarks: $30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$, $45° = \frac{\pi}{4}$, $60° = \frac{\pi}{3}$, $90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$, $180° = \pi$, $270° = \frac{3\pi}{2}$, $360° = 2\pi$. Read the denominators as 'how many slices fit in a half-turn.'

The payoff formula: **arc length $s = r\theta$** ($\theta$ in radians) — an angle times a radius gives a distance, with no conversion constant. In degrees the same formula needs $\frac{\pi}{180}$ glued on; radians are the unit that makes circle formulas clean, which is why all of higher math speaks them.
lessons.keyIdea: 1 radian = 1 radius of arc; 180° = π converts everything; and s = rθ turns angles into distances with zero fuss.

facts.title: The definition
facts.explanation: The circle measures itself with its own radius.

workedExamples.statement: Convert $135°$ to radians and $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ to degrees, exactly.
workedExamples.solution: $135° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{135\pi}{180} = \frac{3\pi}{4}$. And $\frac{5\pi}{6} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} = \frac{5 \cdot 180}{6} = 150°$. Both are landmark-adjacent: $135° = 90° + 45°$ and $150° = 180° - 30°$ — worth recognizing on sight.

workedExamples.statement: A pendulum of length $6$ m swings through $\frac{\pi}{3}$ radians. How far does its tip travel, exactly?
workedExamples.solution: Arc length: $s = r\theta = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} = 2\pi \approx 6.28$ m. Angle times radius, done — this one-liner is why physicists refuse to work in degrees.

commonMistakes.text: Using s = rθ with the angle in degrees.
commonMistakes.correction: The formula is a RADIAN formula — it comes straight from the definition $\theta = \frac{s}{r}$. With degrees you'd need $s = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180}$. Convert first, then multiply.

commonMistakes.text: Reading π/6 and π/3 as interchangeable 'small angles.'
commonMistakes.correction: The DENOMINATOR counts how many slices fill a half-turn: $\frac{\pi}{6}$ is one of SIX slices ($30°$), $\frac{\pi}{3}$ one of THREE ($60°$) — twice as big. Bigger denominator, smaller angle.

tryIt.statement: Convert $240°$ to radians and $\frac{7\pi}{4}$ to degrees, exactly.
A circular running track has radius $50$ m. How far do you run sweeping a central angle of $\frac{3\pi}{5}$, exactly?

steps.title: Why 360 was never sacred
steps.body: Degrees are Babylonian bureaucracy — 360 chosen four thousand years ago (nice divisors, roughly the days in a year). Mathematics prefers a unit the circle chooses itself: wrap the RADIUS along the rim, and the angle that one radius-length of arc subtends is **one radian**. No culture, no committee — every circle in the universe agrees on it.

steps.title: How many radii around?
steps.teach: Unroll the circumference: exactly $2\pi \approx 6.28$ radius-lengths wrap the full rim. That's the whole conversion story: a full turn is $2\pi$ radians, a half-turn is $\pi$.

steps.title: Wrap radii onto the rim
steps.teach: Step arcs of one radius each around the circle: 1 rad, 2 rad, 3 rad ($\approx 172°$ — just shy of the half-turn), and $\pi \approx 3.14$ rad lands the half-turn exactly. One radian $\approx 57.3°$.

steps.title: One fact converts everything
steps.body: From $180° = \pi$: degrees $\to$ radians is $\times \frac{\pi}{180}$; radians $\to$ degrees is $\times \frac{180}{\pi}$. The landmark angles become fractions of $\pi$: $30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$, $45° = \frac{\pi}{4}$, $60° = \frac{\pi}{3}$, $90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$. Read denominators as slice counts of the half-turn: $\frac{\pi}{6}$ — six slices fit, so it's the small one.

steps.title: Both directions
steps.intro: Multiply by π/180 or 180/π — then simplify the fraction.

steps.title: Slice count
steps.prompt: Which is the LARGER angle: $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ or $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$?
options: $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ — only four of them fill a half-turn
steps.explanation: $\frac{\pi}{4} = 45°$ beats $\frac{\pi}{6} = 30°$. Bigger denominator = more slices fit = SMALLER slice. (Ordinary fraction logic — $\pi$ changes nothing.)

steps.title: s = rθ — angles become distances
steps.body: The definition $\theta = \frac{s}{r}$ flips into the cleanest formula in circle geometry: **arc length $= r\theta$**. A $\frac{\pi}{3}$ sweep on a radius-6 circle covers $6 \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} = 2\pi$ meters. No $\frac{\pi}{180}$ barnacles — THIS is why radians win: they make every circle formula (and later, every calculus formula) drop its conversion constants.

steps.title: The pendulum's sweep
steps.intro: Radius times radian angle — one multiplication.

examples.prompt: A $6$ m pendulum swings through $\frac{\pi}{3}$. Arc traveled?

problems.explanation: $\frac{240\pi}{180} = \frac{4\pi}{3}$ — a half-turn plus a $60°$ slice, deep in the third quadrant.

problems.prompt: A wheel of radius $30$ cm rolls through $\frac{2\pi}{3}$. How far does a rim point travel?

steps.title: What sticks
points: 1 radian = 1 radius of arc; full turn = 2π, half-turn = π (≈ 57.3° each).
Convert through 180° = π; landmark denominators count slices of the half-turn.
s = rθ (radians only): angle × radius = distance, no constants.

lessons.title: The Unit Circle
lessons.concreteComparison: A Ferris wheel of radius 1, boarding at the rightmost point: your position after rotating by θ has a horizontal coordinate and a vertical coordinate. Those two numbers ARE cosine and sine. The wheel doesn't compute them from a triangle — it IS them.
lessons.objective: Define cos θ and sin θ as unit-circle coordinates, evaluate them at the axis angles, and extend trig beyond 90°.
concept: The **unit circle**: radius 1, centered at the origin. Rotate from the positive x-axis by angle $\theta$ (counterclockwise positive) to a point $P$. Definition: $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ — cosine is the x-coordinate, sine is the y-coordinate. For acute $\theta$ this MATCHES the triangle definition (drop the perpendicular: adjacent $= x$, opposite $= y$, hypotenuse $= 1$), but it keeps working for ANY angle: $120°$, $300°$, $-45°$, $750°$.

The axis angles read straight off the coordinates: at $0$: $(1, 0)$; at $\frac{\pi}{2}$: $(0, 1)$; at $\pi$: $(-1, 0)$; at $\frac{3\pi}{2}$: $(0, -1)$. So $\cos\pi = -1$, $\sin\frac{3\pi}{2} = -1$, and so on — no memorizing, just point at the circle.

Two structural facts come free. **Periodicity**: a full extra turn changes nothing, so $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$. **The Pythagorean identity**: $P$ sits on the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 1$, so $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ for every $\theta$ — the circle's equation wearing trig clothes. And $\tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \frac{y}{x}$, the slope of the radius arm.
lessons.keyIdea: On the radius-1 circle, the rotated point IS (cos θ, sin θ) — a definition that works for every angle, makes the axis values readable, and hands you cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 for free.

facts.title: The definition
facts.explanation: Cosine = x, sine = y, on the radius-1 circle.

facts.title: Axis values
facts.explanation: Read them off the circle — never memorize.

facts.title: Free theorems
facts.explanation: The circle's equation, and a full turn changing nothing.

workedExamples.solution: Half a turn lands at $(-1, 0)$: $\cos\pi = -1$, $\sin\pi = 0$. Three quarter-turns land at $(0, -1)$: $\cos\frac{3\pi}{2} = 0$, $\sin\frac{3\pi}{2} = -1$. Point at the circle, read the coordinates — that's the entire technique.

workedExamples.statement: The angle $\theta$ puts $P$ at $\left(-\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right)$ on the unit circle. Find $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$, and $\tan\theta$, and verify $P$ really is on the circle.
workedExamples.solution: By definition: $\cos\theta = -\frac{3}{5}$, $\sin\theta = \frac{4}{5}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{4/5}{-3/5} = -\frac{4}{3}$. On-circle check: $\left(-\frac{3}{5}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 = \frac{9 + 16}{25} = 1$ ✓. Negative cosine, positive sine: second quadrant.

commonMistakes.text: Assigning sine to x and cosine to y.
commonMistakes.correction: Cosine is the x-coordinate (alphabetical luck: C before S, x before y). Anchor: at $\theta = 0$ the point is $(1, 0)$ — all the way right — and $\cos 0 = 1$ ✓. One anchored example beats any mnemonic.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking trig values beyond 90° are undefined because no right triangle exists.
commonMistakes.correction: The unit circle REPLACES the triangle definition precisely to escape that limit: any rotation — $120°$, $-30°$, $500°$ — lands somewhere on the circle, and its coordinates are the cosine and sine. The triangle was the ladder; the circle is the building.

tryIt.solution: A full turn returns to $(1, 0)$: $\sin 2\pi = 0$, $\cos 2\pi = 1$. And $\frac{\pi}{2} + 2\pi$ is just $\frac{\pi}{2}$ plus a full lap: $\sin = 1$. Periodicity in action.

tryIt.statement: $\theta$ puts $P$ at $\left(\frac{5}{13}, -\frac{12}{13}\right)$. Find $\tan\theta$ and name the quadrant.
tryIt.solution: $\tan\theta = \frac{-12/13}{5/13} = -\frac{12}{5}$. Positive x, negative y: fourth quadrant. (On-circle: $\frac{25 + 144}{169} = 1$ ✓.)

steps.title: From triangle to circle
steps.body: Right triangles cap $\theta$ below $90°$ — no triangle has a $120°$ 'acute' angle. The escape: put the angle at the center of a radius-1 circle, rotating counterclockwise from the positive x-axis. The rotated point $P$ has coordinates, and we DEFINE $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ to BE those coordinates: $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. For acute angles this agrees perfectly with SOH-CAH-TOA (hypotenuse 1); for every other angle, it's the new law.

steps.title: The coordinates ARE the values
steps.teach: Drive the point around the circle: the x-readout is $\cos\theta$, the y-readout is $\sin\theta$, live in every quadrant. Watch the signs flip as you cross each axis — and watch the little right triangle inside flip with them.

steps.title: Read the axis points
steps.prompt: The angle $\pi$ (half a turn) lands at $(-1, 0)$. What are $\cos\pi$ and $\sin\pi$?
steps.explanation: Cosine is the x-coordinate ($-1$), sine the y ($0$). The four axis angles never need memorizing — point at the circle, read the pair.

steps.title: The Pythagorean identity, geometrically
steps.body: Every point on the unit circle satisfies the circle's equation $x^2 + y^2 = 1$. Substitute the definition: $$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$ for EVERY angle — the single most-used identity in trigonometry, and here it costs nothing: it's just 'P is on the circle.' (For acute angles it's $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ divided by $c^2$ — the same fact in triangle clothes.)

steps.title: A point tells all
steps.intro: Given the landing point, every trig value is a coordinate read.

examples.prompt: $\theta$ lands at $P\left(-\frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5}\right)$. Find $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$, $\tan\theta$; verify $P$ is on the circle.

steps.title: Laps don't matter
steps.body: Add a full turn ($2\pi$) to any angle and you land on the SAME point — so every trig value repeats: $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$, $\cos(\theta + 2\pi) = \cos\theta$. Sine and cosine are **periodic** with period $2\pi$. This one fact turns them from triangle tools into the mathematics of everything that repeats — tides, sound, seasons, circuits. Unit 4 draws the consequences.

steps.title: Strip the laps
steps.explanation: $4\pi$ = two complete turns: the point lands exactly where plain $\frac{\pi}{3}$ does, and $\cos\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{1}{2}$. First move on any big angle: subtract whole laps.

problems.explanation: Three quarter-turns point straight DOWN: $(0, -1)$, and sine reads the y-coordinate: $-1$.
$\tan = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{-12/13}{5/13} = -\frac{12}{5}$ — fourth quadrant, where tangent runs negative.

problems.prompt: Which equation does EVERY unit-circle angle satisfy?
problems.explanation: It's the circle's own equation $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ with the definition substituted. (The middle option fails already at $45°$: $\frac{\sqrt2}{2} + \frac{\sqrt2}{2} = \sqrt2 \neq 1$.)

steps.title: Your GPS rides the unit circle
steps.body: Every satellite position, every phone-screen rotation, every game character turning smoothly — computed as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ pairs, millions of times per second. The unit circle is arguably the single most-executed piece of mathematics on Earth right now.

steps.title: What sticks
points: P(θ) = (cos θ, sin θ) on the radius-1 circle — every angle allowed.
Axis angles read off coordinates: π → (−1, 0), 3π/2 → (0, −1).
Free theorems: cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 (the circle's equation) and 2π-periodicity (laps don't matter).

lessons.title: Reference Angles & Signs
lessons.concreteComparison: A city with four districts and one master key: every address in town is a mirrored copy of an address in the first district. To evaluate anything anywhere, find its first-district twin (the reference angle), look up the value there, then let the district decide the sign.
lessons.objective: Determine the sign of each trig function by quadrant, find reference angles, and reduce any angle's evaluation to a first-quadrant lookup.
concept: Signs come from coordinates. Quadrant I: $x > 0, y > 0$ — everything positive. QII: $x < 0$ — only sine positive. QIII: both negative — only tangent positive ($\frac{-}{-} = +$). QIV: $y < 0$ — only cosine positive. The classic mnemonic reads counterclockwise: **A**ll, **S**ine, **T**angent, **C**osine ('All Students Take Calculus').

The **reference angle** $\theta_{ref}$ is the acute angle between the terminal arm and the X-AXIS (never the y-axis): QII: $\pi - \theta$; QIII: $\theta - \pi$; QIV: $2\pi - \theta$. By the circle's mirror symmetry, $\theta$ and its reference angle have the SAME trig values except possibly for sign.

The universal recipe: (1) strip whole laps; (2) find the quadrant → sign (ASTC); (3) find the reference angle → magnitude (from the Unit 2 table); (4) combine. Example: $\sin\frac{5\pi}{6}$ — QII (sine positive), reference $\pi - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}$, so $\sin\frac{5\pi}{6} = +\sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$.
lessons.keyIdea: Quadrant gives the sign (All-Sine-Tangent-Cosine), the reference angle gives the magnitude — every evaluation reduces to a first-quadrant lookup.

facts.title: ASTC
facts.explanation: Read counterclockwise; from the coordinate signs.

facts.title: Reference angle
facts.explanation: The acute gap to the x-axis — always the x-axis.

facts.title: The recipe
facts.explanation: Any angle, three steps, one table lookup.

workedExamples.solution: $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ ($150°$) is QII: sine positive, cosine negative. Reference angle: $\pi - \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}$. So $\sin\frac{5\pi}{6} = +\sin\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos\frac{5\pi}{6} = -\cos\frac{\pi}{6} = -\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$.

$\frac{4\pi}{3}$ ($240°$) is QIII: tangent POSITIVE (both coordinates negative, ratio positive). Reference: $\frac{4\pi}{3} - \pi = \frac{\pi}{3}$. So $\tan\frac{4\pi}{3} = +\tan\frac{\pi}{3} = \sqrt{3}$.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring the reference angle to the y-axis when the arm is 'closer' to it.
commonMistakes.correction: Reference angles are ALWAYS measured to the x-axis — that's what makes the mirror symmetry give equal magnitudes. For $\frac{2\pi}{3}$: reference is $\pi - \frac{2\pi}{3} = \frac{\pi}{3}$, not the $\frac{\pi}{6}$ gap to the y-axis.

commonMistakes.text: Applying the sign to the reference angle itself instead of the final value.
commonMistakes.correction: The reference angle is a POSITIVE acute angle — a lookup key, nothing more. The sign belongs to the final answer, decided by the quadrant: $\cos\frac{5\pi}{6} = -\cos\frac{\pi}{6}$, the minus applied at assembly.

tryIt.solution: $\frac{2\pi}{3}$: QII, cosine negative, reference $\frac{\pi}{3}$: $-\cos\frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{1}{2}$. $\frac{7\pi}{6}$: QIII, sine negative, reference $\frac{7\pi}{6} - \pi = \frac{\pi}{6}$: $-\sin\frac{\pi}{6} = -\frac{1}{2}$.

tryIt.statement: $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\cos\theta < 0$. Which quadrant is $\theta$ in — and what is the sign of $\tan\theta$ there?
tryIt.solution: Positive sine (upper half) with negative cosine (left half): QII. There $\tan = \frac{+}{-} < 0$ — negative. (Check with $\frac{2\pi}{3}$: $\tan\frac{2\pi}{3} = -\sqrt3$ ✓.)

steps.title: Signs come from coordinates
steps.body: Cosine IS x and sine IS y — so their signs are just the quadrant's coordinate signs. QI: $(+,+)$, all three functions positive. QII: $(-,+)$, only sine survives. QIII: $(-,-)$, only tangent (negative over negative). QIV: $(+,-)$, only cosine. Counterclockwise: **All, Sine, Tangent, Cosine** — the ASTC compass.

steps.title: Reference angles: the first-quadrant twin
steps.body: Fold the circle across the axes and every point lands on a first-quadrant twin. The **reference angle** is the acute gap between the arm and the X-AXIS: QII: $\pi - \theta$; QIII: $\theta - \pi$; QIV: $2\pi - \theta$. Mirrors preserve distances, so $\theta$ and its twin share the same coordinate MAGNITUDES — same trig values up to sign. One table (Unit 2) now covers the whole circle.

steps.title: Sign first
steps.prompt: Without computing: what is the SIGN of $\cos 200°$?
options: Negative — $200°$ is in QIII, where only tangent is positive
steps.explanation: $200°$ sits between $180°$ and $270°$: QIII, coordinates $(-,-)$. Cosine (the x-coordinate) is negative. The sign NEVER depends on the reference angle — quadrant decides sign, reference decides size.

steps.title: Find the twin
steps.prompt: What is the reference angle of $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$?
steps.explanation: $\frac{7\pi}{4} = 315°$ is QIV: reference $= 2\pi - \frac{7\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}$. So e.g. $\cos\frac{7\pi}{4} = +\frac{\sqrt2}{2}$ (QIV keeps cosine positive).

steps.title: Around the circle
steps.intro: Run the recipe: laps, quadrant, reference, assemble.

steps.title: Sketch the arm, every time
steps.body: A two-second sketch — circle, arm in roughly the right quadrant — makes both the sign and the reference angle VISIBLE before any formula runs. Nearly every quadrant error in existence comes from skipping the sketch. Don't be proud; draw the arm.

steps.title: What sticks
points: ASTC counterclockwise: All, Sine, Tangent, Cosine positive — straight from coordinate signs.
Reference angle = acute gap to the x-axis: π−θ, θ−π, 2π−θ by quadrant.
Recipe: strip laps → quadrant (sign) → reference (magnitude) → assemble.

lessons.title: Exact Values Around the Circle
lessons.concreteComparison: Unit 2 gave you five landmark keys on the piano; the circle's symmetry now tunes the whole keyboard from them. Sixteen angles around the rim — every multiple of 30° and 45° — and you can play each one exactly, because each is a mirrored landmark.
lessons.objective: Evaluate sin, cos, and tan exactly at any multiple of π/6 or π/4, in any quadrant, including negative angles.
concept: The full tour: every multiple of $\frac{\pi}{6}$ ($30°$) and $\frac{\pi}{4}$ ($45°$) around the circle reduces by the three-step recipe to a Unit-2 landmark. $\sin\frac{5\pi}{3}$: QIV, reference $\frac{\pi}{3}$, sine negative there: $-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$. Sixteen special points, five memorized values, one recipe.

**Negative angles** rotate clockwise. Symmetry across the x-axis gives the parity rules: $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$ (x survives the flip — cosine is EVEN) and $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ (y flips — sine is ODD); hence $\tan(-\theta) = -\tan\theta$. So $\cos(-\frac{\pi}{3}) = \frac{1}{2}$, $\sin(-\frac{\pi}{6}) = -\frac{1}{2}$.

Big angles strip down: subtract $2\pi$ until you land in $[0, 2\pi)$ — $\frac{13\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6} + 2\pi$, so all its values match $\frac{\pi}{6}$'s. The full skill: ANY multiple of $30°$ or $45°$, positive, negative, or huge, evaluated exactly in three moves.
lessons.keyIdea: Any multiple of π/6 or π/4 — negative or huge — reduces to a landmark: strip laps, apply even/odd for minus signs, then quadrant + reference.

facts.title: Even & odd
facts.explanation: Flip across the x-axis: x survives, y negates.

facts.title: The count
facts.explanation: Symmetry does the memorizing for you.

workedExamples.solution: $\frac{5\pi}{3} = 300°$: QIV — cosine positive, sine and tangent negative. Reference: $2\pi - \frac{5\pi}{3} = \frac{\pi}{3}$. Values: $\sin = -\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$, $\cos = +\frac{1}{2}$, $\tan = -\sqrt3$. (Consistency: $\tan = \frac{\sin}{\cos} = \frac{-\sqrt3/2}{1/2} = -\sqrt3$ ✓.)

commonMistakes.text: Treating cosine like sine under negation: cos(−θ) = −cos θ.
commonMistakes.correction: Picture the flip across the x-axis: the x-coordinate DOESN'T move — cosine is even, $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$. Only the y (sine) negates. Test on $\theta = 0$: $\cos 0 = 1$, and $-1$ would be absurd.

commonMistakes.text: Evaluating big angles by feeding them straight into the quadrant recipe.
commonMistakes.correction: The quadrant formulas assume $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$. Strip whole laps FIRST: $\frac{17\pi}{6} - 2\pi = \frac{5\pi}{6}$, THEN run quadrant + reference. Skipping the strip mislocates the quadrant.

tryIt.solution: $\frac{7\pi}{6}$: QIII, reference $\frac{\pi}{6}$, cosine negative: $-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$. $\frac{11\pi}{6}$: QIV, reference $\frac{\pi}{6}$, tangent negative: $-\frac{\sqrt3}{3}$.

steps.title: Sixteen points, five values
steps.body: Mark every multiple of $\frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\frac{\pi}{4}$ on the rim: sixteen special points. Each one is a MIRRORED landmark — same coordinates as a first-quadrant angle, up to sign. You memorized five values in Unit 2; symmetry now hands you all sixty-four (16 angles × 4 functions... minus the undefined tangents). This lesson is pure execution of the recipe.

steps.title: A full workup at 300°
steps.intro: One angle, all three functions, with the internal consistency check.

steps.title: Negative angles: even cosine, odd sine
steps.body: A negative angle rotates CLOCKWISE — the mirror image across the x-axis of its positive twin. The x-coordinate survives the flip: $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$ (cosine is **even**). The y-coordinate negates: $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ (sine is **odd**). Tangent inherits sine's flip. Two symmetry rules, and the negative half of the number line comes free.

steps.title: The flip
options: $\dfrac{1}{2}$ — cosine is even; the minus evaporates
steps.explanation: Flipping across the x-axis leaves x untouched: $\cos(-\frac{\pi}{3}) = \cos\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{1}{2}$. Had it been SINE, the minus would stay: $\sin(-\frac{\pi}{3}) = -\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$.

steps.title: Minus signs and extra laps
steps.intro: Parity rules for the minus, lap-stripping for the excess.

steps.title: The grand tour
steps.intro: Every trick at once: laps, minus signs, quadrants, references.

steps.title: The circle in your game controller
steps.body: Every analog-stick reading is a point near the unit circle: tilt direction = angle, tilt amount = radius, and games decode it with exactly the quadrant-and-sign logic you just practiced. Aim down-left and the code sees negative cosine, negative sine — QIII, verbatim from this lesson.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Sixteen special points all reduce to five landmarks via quadrant + reference.
cos is even, sin is odd: the minus evaporates or escapes accordingly.
Big angles: strip 2π laps first, THEN run the recipe.

practice.statement: Convert exactly: $150°$ to radians, and $\frac{5\pi}{4}$ to degrees.
A sector of a circle with radius $9$ has central angle $\frac{2\pi}{3}$. Find its arc length, exactly.
$\theta$ lands at $\left(-\frac{8}{17}, -\frac{15}{17}\right)$ on the unit circle. Find all three trig values and the quadrant.
practice.solution: $\cos\theta = -\frac{8}{17}$, $\sin\theta = -\frac{15}{17}$, $\tan\theta = \frac{15}{8}$. Both coordinates negative: QIII (where tangent is positive ✓). On-circle: $\frac{64 + 225}{289} = 1$ ✓.
$\frac{11\pi}{6}$: QIV, reference $\frac{\pi}{6}$, cos positive: $\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$. $\frac{4\pi}{3}$: QIII, reference $\frac{\pi}{3}$, sin negative: $-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$.

practice.statement: In which quadrant is $\theta$ if $\tan\theta > 0$ and $\sin\theta < 0$? Give one such angle exactly and verify.
practice.solution: Tangent positive: QI or QIII; sine negative rules out QI: **QIII**. Example: $\frac{4\pi}{3}$ — $\tan\frac{4\pi}{3} = \sqrt3 > 0$ and $\sin\frac{4\pi}{3} = -\frac{\sqrt3}{2} < 0$ ✓.
Strip a lap: $\frac{17\pi}{6} - 2\pi = \frac{5\pi}{6}$. QII, reference $\frac{\pi}{6}$, sine positive: $\frac{1}{2}$.

testYourself.statement: A wiper blade of length $40$ cm sweeps $\frac{3\pi}{4}$ radians. How far does its tip travel, exactly — and how many degrees is the sweep?
For which two angles in $[0, 2\pi)$ is $\cos\theta = 0$ — and what happens to $\tan\theta$ there?
testYourself.solution: Cosine (the x-coordinate) is 0 at the top and bottom of the circle: $\frac{\pi}{2}$ and $\frac{3\pi}{2}$. Tangent $= \frac{\sin}{\cos}$ divides by zero: UNDEFINED at both — the two vertical asymptotes of Unit 4's tangent graph.

$\frac{\sqrt3}{2} + \left(-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\right) = 0$. The two angles mirror across the x-axis... precisely: they're supplement-and-reflection partners whose sines cancel — visible instantly on a sketch.

testYourself.statement: Given $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$ with $\theta$ in QII, find $\cos\theta$ and $\tan\theta$ exactly.
testYourself.solution: Pythagorean identity: $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{9}{25} = \frac{16}{25}$, so $\cos\theta = \pm\frac{4}{5}$ — and QII forces the NEGATIVE: $\cos\theta = -\frac{4}{5}$. Then $\tan\theta = \frac{3/5}{-4/5} = -\frac{3}{4}$.

testYourself.statement: Order from smallest to largest, no calculator: $\cos\frac{\pi}{6}$, $\cos\frac{5\pi}{6}$, $\cos\frac{\pi}{3}$.
Evaluate exactly: $\cos\left(-\frac{13\pi}{4}\right)$ (strip laps AND use parity).
testYourself.solution: Even first: $\cos(-\frac{13\pi}{4}) = \cos\frac{13\pi}{4}$. Strip a lap: $\frac{13\pi}{4} - 2\pi = \frac{5\pi}{4}$. QIII, reference $\frac{\pi}{4}$, cosine negative: $-\frac{\sqrt2}{2}$.
